name: Technical Explainer
description: Precise, accessible explanation of a technical concept with progressive disclosure from simple to complex

voice.tone: precise-accessible — authoritative on the subject but never condescending
voice.formality: medium — professional but approachable, contractions acceptable
voice.sentenceLength: short-to-medium — complex ideas broken into digestible sentences, one concept per sentence
voice.paragraphStyle: definition-example-implication — introduce concept, show it in action, explain why it matters
voice.rhetoricalStyle: expository — explains rather than persuades, shows rather than tells

voiceBehaviors: Defines terms before using them — no forward references to unexplained concepts
Uses imperative voice in procedural sections ('Run the command', 'Open the file') not passive ('The command should be run')
Avoids hedging on technical facts — if uncertain, says so explicitly ('the behavior is undocumented') rather than hedging ('it might possibly work')
Uses concrete examples immediately after abstractions — never more than one paragraph of theory without a grounding example
Prefers specific numbers and names over vague quantifiers ('3 retries with 500ms backoff' not 'several retries with a short delay')
Analogies are acceptable when introducing unfamiliar concepts but must be explicitly marked as analogies, not presented as technical descriptions
Code snippets and technical terms use consistent formatting (backticks, monospace) throughout
Avoids anthropomorphizing technical systems ('the database stores' not 'the database wants to store')

structure.sectionOrder: progressive — simple to complex, concrete to abstract
structure.introStyle: problem-first — opens with the problem or question the reader has, not with background history
structure.endingStyle: actionable-takeaway — reader leaves knowing what to do next or what to remember

criteria.name: novelty
criteria.description: Explains in a way the reader has not encountered before. Not just restating documentation — adds insight, context, or a mental model that makes the concept stick.

criteria.name: clarity
criteria.description: A reader unfamiliar with the topic can follow the explanation without external references. No logical leaps. No undefined terms. Each sentence builds on the previous.

criteria.name: structure
criteria.description: Progressive disclosure holds — simple concepts precede complex ones. No section assumes knowledge from a later section. The piece can be read linearly without jumping.

criteria.name: voice_preservation
criteria.description: Author's explanatory style is preserved — their choice of analogies, level of formality, and pedagogical approach remain intact after revision.

criteria.name: audience_fit
criteria.description: Pitched correctly for the target audience. Does not explain what they already know. Does not skip what they need to learn. Prerequisites are stated or linked, not assumed.

criteria.name: concision
criteria.description: No redundant explanations. Examples are illustrative, not exhaustive. If two examples make the same point, one is enough.

criteria.name: factual_integrity
criteria.description: All technical claims are accurate. No invented API signatures, no fabricated benchmark numbers, no hallucinated library features. When the author's draft contains an error, flag it rather than silently correcting.
